name: Kshama Prarthana — Forgiveness Prayer
when_to_chant: At the end of any puja, ritual, or sankalp — especially if steps were missed, mistakes were made, or the ritual could not be completed fully
importance: In Hindu tradition, it is accepted that humans are imperfect. The Kshama Prarthana acknowledges this and asks the deity for forgiveness for any errors — of action, speech, mind, or omission. Chanting this with sincerity completes any ritual regardless of mistakes.

verses.sanskrit:
आवाहनं न जानामि न जानामि विसर्जनम् ।
पूजां चैव न जानामि क्षमस्व परमेश्वर ॥
verses.iast:
āvāhanaṃ na jānāmi na jānāmi visarjanam |
pūjāṃ caiva na jānāmi kṣamasva parameśvara ||
verses.english_meaning: I do not know how to invoke You, nor how to bid You farewell. I do not know how to worship You properly. O Supreme Lord, please forgive me.

verses.sanskrit:
मन्त्रहीनं क्रियाहीनं भक्तिहीनं सुरेश्वर ।
यत्पूजितं मया देव परिपूर्णं तदस्तु मे ॥
verses.iast:
mantrahīnaṃ kriyāhīnaṃ bhaktihīnaṃ sureśvara |
yatpūjitaṃ mayā deva paripūrṇaṃ tadastu me ||
verses.english_meaning: O Lord of the Gods, my worship may have been without the proper mantras, without the proper actions, and without true devotion — yet may the puja I have offered to You be made complete and perfect.

word_meanings.tadastu me: may that be so for me

verses.sanskrit:
अपराध सहस्राणि क्रियन्तेऽहर्निशं मया ।
दासोऽयमिति मां मत्वा क्षमस्व परमेश्वर ॥
verses.iast:
aparādha sahasrāṇi kriyante'harniśaṃ mayā |
dāso'yamiti māṃ matvā kṣamasva parameśvara ||
verses.english_meaning: Thousands of offences are committed by me day and night. Considering that I am Your servant, O Supreme Lord, please forgive me.

how_to_chant: Sit quietly after completing the puja or ritual
Fold your hands (Anjali mudra) with palms pressed together at the heart
Close your eyes and speak from the heart — the words matter less than the sincerity
Chant all three verses slowly, once each
After the last verse, bow your head and silently ask the deity for completion of the ritual
significance: The Kshama Prarthana is the safety net of every Hindu ritual. The tradition acknowledges that we are human — we forget words, we rush, our minds wander, or life circumstances prevent full completion. This prayer says: 'I tried my best. Please accept what I offered and make it whole.' The scriptures assure that a sincere Kshama Prarthana makes any imperfect puja complete in God's eyes.
